ref.title: Should private banks be allowed to create money?

feed_card.headline: In the UK, banks create about 97% of all money, and only 3% is cash the state issued.

teasers: Your deposit is not cash the bank stored
A 2014 experiment caught a bank creating money
Why paying off a loan deletes money

sections.content: In the UK, banks create about 97% of all money, and only 3% is cash the state issued.

content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='28' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='15' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>The instant a bank makes a 100 pound loan</text><rect x='28' y='66' width='158' height='70' rx='14' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.55'/><text x='107' y='90' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='600' letter-spacing='0.06em' opacity='0.6' text-anchor='middle'>WHAT THE BANK OWNS</text><text x='107' y='118' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='17' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>+100 loan</text><text x='200' y='107' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='20' font-weight='700' opacity='0.5' text-anchor='middle'>=</text><rect x='214' y='66' width='158' height='70' rx='14' fill='var(--accent)' fill-opacity='0.12' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='293' y='90' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='600' letter-spacing='0.06em' text-anchor='middle'>WHAT IT OWES YOU</text><text x='293' y='118' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='17' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>+100 deposit</text><text x='200' y='168' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.7' text-anchor='middle'>Both appear at once. The deposit is new money,</text><text x='200' y='186' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.7' text-anchor='middle'>not money moved from somewhere else.</text></svg>

sections.content: Picture what a bank does with your savings. The natural assumption is that it keeps your deposit somewhere safe and lends it on to someone else, passing a fixed pile of money from savers to borrowers. That picture is wrong. When a bank grants a loan it hands over no one else's money. It writes a new number into the borrower's account. The loan the bank now owns and the deposit it now owes appear together on its books, and that deposit is money that did not exist a second earlier. The balances sitting in everyone's accounts are mostly not stored cash, they are debts the banks brought into being by lending.

content.body: For most of the twentieth century, students learned the reverse. The standard textbook taught the money multiplier: a bank takes in a deposit, keeps a fraction in reserve, lends the rest, that loan returns to the system as a fresh deposit, and the cycle repeats until a small base of central bank money has been multiplied into a much larger stock. Joseph Schumpeter had already objected. In his History of Economic Analysis, published in 1954, four years after his death, he wrote that it is far more realistic to say banks 'create deposits in their act of lending' than to say they 'lend the deposits that have been entrusted to them'. The tidy multiplier model won the classroom anyway. Two events in 2014 finally closed the argument. The Bank of England gave a Quarterly Bulletin article over to stating plainly that commercial banks create money by making loans, and that the multiplier story is a misconception. The same year the economist Richard Werner ran the first clean test: he borrowed 200,000 euros from a small German cooperative, Raiffeisenbank Wildenberg, while staff tracked the internal records, and confirmed the bank created the deposit on the spot rather than shifting it from any other account.

key_figures.name: Joseph Schumpeter
key_figures.one_line: He wrote in 1954 that a bank creating a deposit when it lends describes the system better than a bank lending out money other people deposited.

key_figures.name: Richard Werner
key_figures.one_line: In 2014 he borrowed from a cooperating German bank and recorded it creating the loan as a new deposit, the first clean empirical test of how banking works.

content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='30' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='15' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>When the textbooks caught up</text><line x1='40' y1='100' x2='360' y2='100' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.5' stroke-linecap='round'/><circle cx='80' cy='100' r='4' fill='currentColor'/><text x='80' y='84' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='14' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>1954</text><text x='80' y='126' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.85' text-anchor='middle'>Schumpeter</text><text x='205' y='150' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.6' text-anchor='middle'>multiplier era</text><circle cx='330' cy='100' r='5.5' fill='var(--accent)'/><text x='330' y='82' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='14' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>2014</text><text x='330' y='126' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>confirmed</text></svg>

content.title: Paying off a loan quietly destroys money
content.body: Money creation runs in reverse too. When you repay a bank loan, the bank cancels the deposit against the loan, and that money stops existing, the same way it appeared when the loan was first made. In months when households and firms across a country repay more debt than they take on, the total money supply can shrink. The pool of money in an economy is not a fixed reservoir. It rises and falls with how much borrowing is happening.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 185' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='30' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='15' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>The same money, created then destroyed</text><rect x='18' y='78' width='104' height='58' rx='14' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2'/><text x='70' y='112' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' text-anchor='middle'>Bank lends</text><rect x='148' y='78' width='104' height='58' rx='14' fill='var(--accent)' fill-opacity='0.12' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='200' y='104' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='16' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>+100</text><text x='200' y='124' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>money created</text><rect x='278' y='78' width='104' height='58' rx='14' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2'/><text x='330' y='104' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' text-anchor='middle'>Loan repaid</text><text x='330' y='124' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='12' opacity='0.8' text-anchor='middle'>money gone</text><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.55'><path d='M122 107 h26'/><path d='M148 107 l-12 -6 m12 6 l-12 6'/><path d='M252 107 h26'/><path d='M278 107 l-12 -6 m12 6 l-12 6'/></g></svg>

content.title: Quantitative easing makes a different kind of money
content.body: When a central bank does quantitative easing, it creates money too, but not the kind in your account. It conjures central bank reserves, an electronic balance that only banks hold, and uses them to buy bonds from financial firms. That can flood the banking system with reserves while the deposits ordinary people spend barely move, which is part of why a decade of QE after 2009 did far less to push up everyday prices than its critics feared. Two separate types of money sit in the system, and only one of them buys groceries.

content.title: No bank can create money without limit
content.body: If banks make money by lending, why not lend forever? Because every new loan is a bet that has to be repaid, and a bank that lends recklessly drowns in bad debts and loses customers to its rivals. Capital and liquidity rules cap how far a bank can stretch, and the central bank sets the interest rate that makes borrowing cheap or dear. Banks create the bulk of the money, but the brakes are real, and working those brakes is most of what monetary policy does.

sections.content: So the money in a modern economy is mostly private credit, brought into being by thousands of commercial banks deciding, loan by loan, who is worth lending to. That gives those decisions a long reach: when banks lend freely the money supply swells, and when they turn cautious it contracts, which is why credit booms and busts move the whole economy and not only the people borrowing. The central bank, the institution most people imagine controls the money, mainly sets the price of borrowing and supervises the system. It does not create most of the money itself. Seeing that changes where you look for the engine of an economy, away from the mint and the printing press and toward the lending desks of ordinary banks.

content.question: When a bank grants you a loan, where does the money in your new deposit come from?
options: The bank creates it as a new deposit on its own books
Another customer's savings, transferred to you
The central bank's reserves, lent through the bank
Physical cash the bank keeps in its vault
content.explanation: The loan and the deposit are created together, as a matching asset and liability. No existing money is moved, and the deposit is new money the act of lending brings into being.

content.question: Why can the total amount of money in an economy fall in a given month?
options: The central bank physically removes banknotes
People repay more loans than they take out, destroying deposits
Banks move their money abroad
Inflation lowers the value of each pound
content.explanation: Repaying a loan cancels the deposit that the loan created, removing that money from circulation. When repayment outpaces new borrowing, the money supply shrinks.

content.question: What was wrong with the textbook 'money multiplier' story?
options: It overstated how much cash people carry
It applied only to central banks
It reversed the real order, since lending creates deposits rather than deposits enabling lending
It left coins out of the count
content.explanation: The multiplier model has banks lending out a base of reserves and deposits. In practice a bank creates a deposit when it lends, and the reserves to settle it are arranged afterward, so the causation runs the other way.

content.question: Quantitative easing creates which kind of money?
options: Banknotes handed directly to households
Central bank reserves, held only by banks
New customer deposits in everyday accounts
Foreign currency reserves
content.explanation: QE credits banks' reserve accounts at the central bank in order to buy bonds. Those reserves circulate only among banks, which is why QE can swell the money base without directly raising the deposits people spend.

content.question: If banks profit by lending, what stops one from creating money endlessly?
options: A legal cap on the number of loans per year
Nothing, which is why banks can create unlimited money
Bad-debt risk, competition, capital and liquidity rules, and the central bank's interest rate
The supply of gold backing each loan
content.explanation: Every loan has to be repaid, so reckless lending means losses. Regulation limits how far a bank can stretch, and the central bank's rate makes borrowing cheaper or dearer, which is the main lever of monetary policy.

content.question: What does the 97% figure imply about who controls a country's money supply?
options: The central bank creates almost all of it
Commercial banks' lending decisions create most of it
The government creates it through taxation
Most money is physical cash in circulation
content.explanation: If 97% of money is bank deposits created through lending, then commercial banks, not the central bank or the treasury, bring most money into existence. The central bank mainly influences the price and pace of that lending.

content.myth: Banks lend out the money their customers deposit.
content.reality: A bank creates a fresh deposit when it lends. Your balance is a record of what the bank owes you, not a sum it ships off to the borrower.

content.myth: The government prints all the money.
content.reality: The state issues notes and coin, only about 3% of the money in the UK, with Bank of England notes worth roughly 82 billion pounds in early 2024. Commercial banks create the other 97% as deposits when they lend.

content.myth: Banks gather reserves first and multiply them into loans.
content.reality: Lending comes first and creates the deposit. The reserves needed to settle it are obtained afterward, so the famous multiplier runs backward.

content.myth: Exactly 97% of money everywhere is bank-created.
content.reality: The 97 to 3 split is the UK figure. The exact share differs by country and shifts over time, though bank deposits dwarf physical cash in every advanced economy.

content.body: That private banks create most of the money is settled. Whether they should is not. If money creation is really a public function being carried out by profit-seeking firms, some hold that the state ought to reclaim it, while others warn that taking the power from banks could starve the economy of credit. The question is live in policy, not only in theory.
items: Should only the central bank be allowed to create money, as the Swiss 'sovereign money' referendum proposed in 2018 before voters rejected it by about 76%?
Would a central bank digital currency, by letting households hold central bank money directly, drain the deposits that fund private money creation?
Does tying the money supply to private lending make financial booms and busts deeper than they would otherwise be?

content.label: McLeay M, Radia A, Thomas R. Money creation in the modern economy. Bank of England Quarterly Bulletin 2014 Q1, 54(1):14-27
Werner RA. Can banks individually create money out of nothing? The theories and the empirical evidence. International Review of Financial Analysis 2014;36:1-19
Schumpeter JA. History of Economic Analysis. Oxford University Press, 1954
